parts.content:
Write 1 dissimilar SBA question(s) on: 'During endovascular treatment of aneurysmal subarachnoid haemorrhage, cerebral autoregulation may be impaired and hypotension can rapidly cause cerebral ischaemia.'.

Each question should test application of knowledge, not simple recall — a candidate should need to reason from principles rather than just retrieve a fact. Use the retrieve tool to find relevant reference material before writing. Return the result as a QuestionSet.

parts.args: {"query": 'During endovascular treatment of aneurysmal subarachnoid haemorrhage, cerebral autoregulation may be impaired and hypotension can rapidly cause cerebral ischaemia.'}

{"topic":"Final Frca Sample Sbas","exam":"final","curriculum_node_code":null,"model":"openai:gpt-5.5","generated_at":"2026-05-02T00:00:00Z","questions":[{"stem":"A 58-year-old woman presents 18 hours after sudden-onset headache and collapse. CT confirms diffuse subarachnoid haemorrhage without hydrocephalus and CT angiography demonstrates a 6 mm anterior communicating artery aneurysm. She is undergoing endovascular coiling under general anaesthesia in the neurointerventional suite. She is GCS 15, has received oral nimodipine, has arterial and large-bore intravenous access, and during the case her MAP falls to 55 mmHg with an EtCO2 of 4.3 kPa as the microcatheter is about to cross the aneurysm neck.","lead":"Which action is most appropriate to optimise cerebral physiology at this point?","options":[{"text":"Administer labetalol to blunt potential BP surges during microcatheter passage","is_correct":false,"explanation":"Labetalol would worsen the current hypotension and reduce cerebral perfusion pressure. Although hypertension may increase rebleeding risk before aneurysm securing, this immediate problem is inadequate MAP."},{"text":"Administer mannitol 0.5 g/kg IV to reduce cerebral swelling","is_correct":false,"explanation":"There is no evidence of raised intracranial pressure, hydrocephalus or cerebral swelling in the scenario. Mannitol may also worsen hypotension through osmotic diuresis and reduce cerebral perfusion."},{"text":"Hyperventilate the patient to EtCO2 of 3.5 kPa to reduce cerebral blood volume","is_correct":false,"explanation":"Hyperventilation causes cerebral vasoconstriction and can reduce cerebral blood flow. In a patient with SAH and impaired autoregulation, this may worsen ischaemia."},{"text":"Reduce anaesthetic depth to increase BP and allow cerebral vasodilation","is_correct":false,"explanation":"Lightening anaesthesia is unpredictable and risks awareness, movement and haemodynamic surges during a critical neurointerventional step. Vasopressor support is a more controlled method of restoring cerebral perfusion pressure."},{"text":"Start phenylephrine infusion","is_correct":true,"explanation":"Phenylephrine will increase MAP and support cerebral perfusion pressure while avoiding unnecessary cerebral vasodilation or hypercapnia. Maintaining adequate CPP is critical during aneurysm manipulation after SAH."}],"explanation":"During endovascular treatment of aneurysmal subarachnoid haemorrhage, cerebral autoregulation may be impaired and hypotension can rapidly cause cerebral ischaemia. With an unsecured aneurysm, extreme hypertension should be avoided, but a MAP of 55 mmHg is inadequate. Normocapnia and restoration of MAP using a vasopressor such as phenylephrine are appropriate; hyperventilation, mannitol or beta-blockade would risk worsening cerebral perfusion.","curriculum_node_codes":["2_GA_C_5","2_GA_M_8","2_RT_G_3","2_GA_C_4","2_GA_N_2"],"curriculum_node_scores":[0.6749444928496313,0.6465697865022388,0.5367042416219316,0.5080567037959369,0.5032783476187603]}]}
